stories.title: Winkler Purchases 94K-SF Broomfield Mixed-Use Property
stories.excerpt: Winkler Development acquired all the properties that make up Mainstreet at FlatIron, a 94,000-square-foot portfolio in Broomfield that includes a two-story retail and office property, a multi-tenant retail building, and a surface parking lot. The price for the package was $19.95 million. Wells Fargo provided a $12 million acquisition loan to help fund the deal. The properties sit across the street from the FlatIron Crossing mall in Broomfield. Mainstreet at FlatIron was constructed in 2002. Aequus Partners was the seller. The group previously purchased the two-building portfolio for $5.08 million in 2017. Mainstreet at FlatIron was constructed in 2002. Last November, Winkler purchased the shopping center of Parker Pavilions, at 11465 S. Parker Road, for $8.4 million. Winkler targets investments that range from $5 million to $80 million. The post Winkler Purchases 94K-SF Broomfield Mixed-Use Property appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Winkler Development acquired 94K-SF Broomfield mixed-use portfolio for $19.95M
Wells Fargo provided $12M acquisition loan; seller Aequus Partners bought property for $5.08M in 2017
Property across from FlatIron Crossing mall; Winkler expanding retail portfolio in Colorado
stories.summary: Winkler Development purchased Mainstreet at FlatIron, a 94,000-square-foot mixed-use property in Broomfield, Colorado, for $19.95 million with $12 million in Wells Fargo financing. The portfolio includes retail, office, and parking across from FlatIron Crossing mall. Aequus Partners, the seller, had acquired the property in 2017 for $5.08 million, reflecting significant value appreciation in the submarket.
stories.impact: Limited relevance to multifamily development. This is a retail/office acquisition targeting value-add in suburban Denver, not a residential play. No direct signal for apartment supply, demand, or financing trends.

stories.title: CREFC Sentiment Index Shows Market Stabilizing into Caution
stories.excerpt: The CRE Finance Council (CREFC) said Monday its Second-Quarter 2026 (2Q26) Board of Governors (BOG) Sentiment Index rose 0.9% to 101.0 from 100.1 in the previous quarter, holding near the survey’s 2017 baseline of 100.0 after the prior quarter’s 20.2% decline. However, Beneath the modest headline move, results were mixed. Five of nine core questions improved – led by the economic outlook – while four softened, led by borrower and investor demand, which moderated from 1Q26’s elevated readings. Conducted between June 25 and July 6, the survey captured a market stabilizing after the geopolitical shock that drove last quarter’s decline, but stabilizing into caution rather than conviction. “After last quarter’s shock, this is what a market catching its breath looks like,” said Lisa Pendergast, president and CEO of CREFC. Neutral was the most common answer on seven of the nine core questions. For example, 58% of respondents now expect the U.S. economy to perform at the same level over the next 12 months, while 24% expect worse performance, down from 54% in the previous survey. Views on interest rates remained the weakest reading for a second consecutive quarter. The post CREFC Sentiment Index Shows Market Stabilizing into Caution appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: CREFC Sentiment Index rose 0.9% to 101.0 in 2Q26 after 20.2% prior quarter decline
Five of nine core questions improved; borrower and investor demand softened from elevated 1Q26 levels
Market stabilizing into caution: 58% expect flat U.S. economy; interest rate views remain weakest reading
stories.summary: The CRE Finance Council's 2Q26 Sentiment Index rebounded slightly to 101.0 from 100.1, recovering from the prior quarter's 20.2% shock-driven decline. Mixed results underneath the modest headline: five core indicators improved (led by economic outlook) while four softened, particularly borrower and investor demand. The survey reflects a market catching its breath but stabilizing into caution, with 58% expecting flat economic performance and interest rate views remaining the weakest metric.
stories.impact: CRITICAL FOR UNDERWRITING AND CAPITAL. Moderating borrower/investor demand signals tightening leverage availability and rising selectivity in multifamily lending. Weak interest rate outlook suggests SOFR volatility will persist, making fixed-rate construction debt expensive and floating-rate refinance risk real. Cautious sentiment may delay leverage-dependent development starts and flag higher cap rates for stabilized acquisitions across all metros—a headwind for both new builds and refinances.

stories.title: Mamdani Selects Underutilized NYPD Parking Lot for Affordable Housing
stories.excerpt: Mayor Zohran Mamdani announced Monday that an underutilized New York Police Department parking lot in Manhattan’s East Village will be redeveloped as mixed-use housing. The site, at 324 East Fifth Street, is slated for approximately 131 affordable housing units, 30 percent of which will be reserved for formerly homeless New Yorkers. The mixed-use project was designated to a development team of Spatial Equity, Housing Works, the Cooper Square Committee and the This Land Is Ours Community Land Trust. The mayor announced the redevelopment plans jointly with Deputy Mayor Leila Bozorg and Dina Levy, head of New York City Housing Preservation and Development. The development, dubbed the Aurea, will include a senior center, community space and replacement parking. Plans also include landscaped terraces, green roofs and all-electric systems. Housing Works will provide on-site support services for residents. On his first day in office this year, Mayor Mamdani mandated a review of city-owned sites that could be used to develop at least 25,000 new housing units over the next decade. The Land Inventory Fast Track (LIFT) task force was created for the purpose. “This project will provide permanently affordable housing, create homes for formerly homeless New Yorkers and put community stewardship at its center through a community land trust,” Mamdani said in a statement. “It’s the first city land designation of our administration, and it’s exactly the kind of housing we’re committed to building across the five boroughs: deeply affordable, community-led and worthy of the greatest city in the world.” Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: NYC Mayor Mamdani announces 131-unit affordable housing on underutilized NYPD parking lot, East Village
30% of units reserved for formerly homeless; development team includes nonprofits and community land trust
Project includes senior center, community space, replacement parking, green roofs, all-electric systems
stories.summary: Mayor Zohran Mamdani announced redevelopment of an underutilized NYPD parking lot at 324 East Fifth Street in Manhattan's East Village into a mixed-use affordable housing project called The Aurea. The 131-unit building will reserve 30% of units for formerly homeless New Yorkers and will include community services, a senior center, and green infrastructure. Development is led by Spatial Equity, Housing Works, Cooper Square Committee, and This Land Is Ours CLT.
stories.impact: MODEST RELEVANCE TO FOR-PROFIT MULTIFAMILY DEVELOPMENT. Public/nonprofit-led affordable housing using city land diverts potential market-rate development sites in tight Manhattan submarkets. The emphasis on homeless services and CLT control signals continued regulatory/political preference for deed-restricted inventory. For-profit developers should monitor East Village entitlement trends—public land transfers may reduce available market-rate opportunities, but ground-lease partnerships with nonprofits could unlock otherwise constrained sites.

stories.title: From the gridiron to the high-rise: Blake Poston planning $100M Uptown Dallas high-rise
stories.excerpt: A former SMU football player is developing a $100 million, 20-story high-rise just north of Uptown Dallas, around a mile from where he used to drop into coverage on Saturdays.  Blake Poston, who was a part of the Southern Methodist University Mustangs football team for three years and went to Highland Park High School, is also the founder of Cover2 Capital. Poston told the Dallas Morning News that Cover2 is aiming to begin construction on an Uptown high-rise at 5220 McKinney Avenue late next year. The building is dubbed The Savannah, and Cover2 is still in design and city development […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Cover2 Capital (founded 2022) planning $100M, 20-story, 100-unit luxury high-rise in Dallas Uptown
The Savannah targets late 2026 construction start; 1,800 SF avg units, 1–3 bedrooms plus three penthouses
Cover2 also developing 297-unit Wheelhouse (North Richland Hills) and garden-style Greerhouse (Anna)
stories.summary: Blake Poston's Cover2 Capital is planning The Savannah, a $100 million, 20-story luxury apartment tower at 5220 McKinney Avenue in Dallas Uptown with 100 units and three penthouses. Average unit size is 1,800 square feet, targeting similar rents to nearby luxury properties. Construction is slated to begin late 2026. Cover2 also operates the 297-unit Wheelhouse mixed-use project in North Richland Hills.
stories.impact: STRONG SIGNAL OF UPTOWN DALLAS MARKET MOMENTUM AND CAPITAL CONFIDENCE. Luxury infill development in constrained Uptown submarket—larger unit sizes and premium positioning reflect strong rental demand and pricing power. Young developer's $100M raise and aggressive timeline suggest capital appetite for high-quality urban multifamily in Dallas. Wheelhouse and Greerhouse completions will test absorbtion and exit strategy; success could unlock future UpMarket pipeline in core Dallas submarkets.

stories.title: Real Estate Firm Landmark Management Signs 12K-SF Lease at One Grand Central Place
stories.excerpt: A real estate investment and management firm is relocating its offices to Midtown East, near Grand Central Terminal, Commercial Observer has learned. Landmark Management signed a 12,168-square-foot lease on the 52nd floor of Empire State Realty Trust (ESRT)’s One Grand Central Place, according to the landlord, in a relocation from its current offices at 115 East 69th Street on the Upper East Side. ESRT did not disclose the asking rent or the length of the lease, but the average office asking rent in Midtown during the second quarter of 2026 was $86.18 per square foot, according to a report from CBRE. Ryan Kass, Jordan Berger, Shanae Ursini and Kerry Lavelle represented ESRT in-house alongside a Newmark team of Scott Klau, William Cohen, Erik Harris and Neil Rubin. CBRE’s Christopher Corrinet and Evan Fiddle negotiated on behalf of the tenant. Newmark and CBRE did not immediately respond to requests for comment. Other tenants in the 55-story building along East 42nd between Madison and Park avenues Street include financial and consulting services firm Gerson Lehrman Group, which expanded to 95,612 square feet there in November 2025, as well as fintech firm iCapital, which expanded to 219,928 square feet in February 2025. J.P. Morgan Chase Bank also operates a large retail location at the base of the building. Chase renewed its 21,683-square-foot lease in February 2026. Mark Hallum can be reached at mhallum@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Landmark Management relocated 12,168-SF office lease to 52nd floor, One Grand Central Place, Midtown East
Midtown average office asking rent $86.18/SF (2Q26); relocation from Upper East Side (115 E. 69th St)
ESRT landlord; Newmark and CBRE brokered transaction
stories.summary: Real estate investment and management firm Landmark Management signed a 12,168-square-foot office lease on the 52nd floor of Empire State Realty Trust's One Grand Central Place in Midtown East, relocating from its Upper East Side offices. Midtown's average office asking rent stands at $86.18 per square foot as of 2Q26. ESRT, Newmark, and CBRE represented all parties.
stories.impact: Minimal relevance to multifamily development. Office leasing activity near Grand Central is a positive sign of Midtown East stability and tenant demand, but does not directly affect apartment supply, demand, or development capital. However, sustained office leasing may support secondary demand for urban rental housing in Midtown/Upper East Side adjacencies.

stories.title: Apollo Provides $71M Loan for Tribeca Luxury Condo Conversion
stories.excerpt: A joint venture between Broad Street Development and TPG Angelo Gordon has secured $71 million in construction financing to build 139 Franklin Street, a boutique residential condominium that will deliver up to 18 units in Manhattan’s Tribeca neighborhood, Commercial Observer can first report. Affiliates of Apollo Global Management provided the construction debt, while a JLL Capital Markets team of Michael Gigliotti and Aaron Niedermayer arranged the transaction. Raymond Chalmé, principal of Broad Street Development, described the new asset as “one of Tribeca’s most distinctive boutique residential offerings,” in a statement and noted that the building is being marketed specifically to luxury buyers in the swanky Manhattan submarket. “We appreciate Apollo’s confidence in our team and JLL’s expertise in arranging financing that positions the development for a successful delivery,” he said. Located near the intersection of Franklin Street, Varick Street and West Broadway, 139 Franklin Street is being built on the site of a 56,384-square-foot, 10-story, 74-unit self-storage facility. The site is in the heart of Tribeca, an enclave of lofts and converted luxury apartments on the west side of Manhattan below Canal Street. Broad Street Development and TPG Angelo Gordon acquired the former site from the Sofia family for $43.5 million in 2025, according to YIMBY. This is far from Broad Street’s first luxury or conversion play in Lower Manhattan. The firm oversaw the construction of 40 Bleecker Street and 215 Sullivan Street in the last decade, and converted the old Maritime Exchange Building into 326 luxury apartments at 80 Broad Street. The new residential condo at 139 Franklin Street is expected to deliver three- to five-bedroom homes with private outdoor space and wellness-style amenities, according to JLL. JLL’s Gigliotti described Tribeca in a statement as one of Manhattan’s most sought-after residential submarket due to the limited invento
bullets: Apollo Global Management funded $71M construction loan for 139 Franklin Street, Tribeca luxury condo conversion
Broad Street Development and TPG Angelo Gordon JV building 18-unit boutique residential condominium
JLL Capital Markets (Gigliotti, Niedermayer) arranged; property near Franklin/Varick/Broadway intersection
stories.summary: A joint venture between Broad Street Development and TPG Angelo Gordon secured $71 million in construction financing from Apollo Global Management for 139 Franklin Street, a boutique luxury condominium project in Tribeca with up to 18 units. The financing was arranged by JLL Capital Markets. The property is positioned as one of Tribeca's most distinctive high-end residential offerings.
stories.impact: Limited relevance to multifamily (rental) development. Luxury condo conversion financing reflects strong capital availability for high-end Manhattan residential and investor appetite for trophy assets. However, condo sales vs. rental apartments serve different investor bases and capital sources. Signal is positive for Manhattan residential fundamentals and trophy-location confidence, but does not inform apartment supply, rents, or multifamily construction debt.

stories.title: Newmark Refis Multifamily Portfolio With $163M Fannie Mae Loan
stories.excerpt: LaSalle Investment Management has sealed $163 million of Fannie Mae-backed debt to refinance three multifamily properties in Washington, D.C., Northern Virginia and Oregon, Commercial Observer has learned. Newmark, a delegated underwriting and servicing (DUS) lender for Fannie Mae, supplied the loan for the 833-unit portfolio. The debt was arranged by Newmark’s Jordan Roeschlaub, Christopher Kramer, Aaron Golesorkhi and Capri Van Gilder. The transaction refinances LaSalle’s existing cross-collateralized Fannie Mae credit facility for the properties while also enabling it to add collateral in the future, according to Newmark. The deal provides “long-term financing while maintaining conservative leverage and strong credit metrics,” Newmark said. The portfolio includes 14W Apartments at 1315 W Street NW in Washington, D.C. Pearson Square at 410 South Maple Avenue in Falls Church, Va.; and Nexus at Orenco Station at 1299 NE Orenco Station Parkway in Hillsboro, Ore. LaSalle Investment Management did not immediately return a request for comment. Andrew Coen can be reached at acoen@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: LaSalle Investment Management refinanced 833-unit multifamily portfolio with $163M Fannie Mae DUS loan via Newmark
Portfolio spans Washington D.C., Northern Virginia, Oregon; includes 14W Apartments (DC), Pearson Square (Falls Church), Nexus at Orenco Station (Hillsboro)
stories.summary: LaSalle Investment Management closed $163 million in Fannie Mae-backed debt arranged by Newmark (DUS lender) to refinance three multifamily properties totaling 833 units across Washington, D.C., Northern Virginia, and Oregon. The deal refinanced LaSalle's existing cross-collateralized credit facility and enables future collateral additions. Properties include 14W Apartments (DC), Pearson Square (Falls Church), and Nexus at Orenco Station (Hillsboro).
stories.impact: POSITIVE SIGNAL FOR MULTIFAMILY CAPITAL AND LEVERAGE. Large-scale GSE refinancing of stabilized portfolio at conservative leverage indicates Fannie Mae's continued appetite for seasoned multifamily, particularly outside hot coastal markets (D.C., Va., Oregon). Refinancing success validates market fundamentals in mid-Atlantic and Pacific Northwest and suggests DUS execution remains smooth. Useful comp for developers targeting stabilized exit financing in these metros.

stories.title: Austin Office Tower Trades for $151M
stories.excerpt: Brandywine Realty Trust sold downtown Austin’s 405 Colorado tower for $151 million. Chris Price at Partners was quoted in LinkedIn saying the buyer was Hines and that the 25-story tower is 100% leased. At its annual meeting, Brandywine’s CEO said the company was looking to divest between $250 million and $300 million in assets. Completed in 2021, 405 Colorado is a 206,000-square-foot office tower with a 520-space, above-ground parking garage located at its namesake address in downtown Austin. The tower was designed by Duda Paine Architects. The Austin Business Journal reports Brandywine has been active in Austin in recent years. It’s developing its sprawling Uptown ATX development adjacent to North Austin’s The Domain and attracting tenants like Nvidia. Last year, Brandywine sold North Austin’s Quarry Lake II, a 120,600-square-foot office building, to Brick Row Holdings. It also sold Southwest Austin’s Four Barton Skyway, a 225,580-square-foot building, to an unknown buyer. The post Austin Office Tower Trades for $151M appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Brandywine Realty Trust sold 405 Colorado (25-story, 206K SF office tower, Austin downtown) to Hines for $151M
Tower 100% leased, completed 2021, includes 520-space parking garage; deal part of $250–300M asset divestiture plan
Brandywine divesting from Austin office while developing Uptown ATX mixed-use (with Nvidia); also sold other Austin assets in 2024
stories.summary: Brandywine Realty Trust sold the fully-leased 405 Colorado office tower in downtown Austin to Hines for $151 million. The 25-story, 206,000-square-foot building was completed in 2021 and includes a 520-space parking garage. The sale is part of Brandywine's broader $250–300 million asset divestiture plan. Brandywine continues mixed-use development at Uptown ATX near The Domain.
stories.impact: INDIRECT SIGNAL FOR AUSTIN MULTIFAMILY MARKET. Brandywine's pivot from stabilized downtown office to mixed-use development (Uptown ATX) reflects developer flight from core office and redeployment into residential-anchored urban development. Hines' acquisition at $151M (≈$733/SF) suggests non-zero life for Class A office with strong credit tenants. However, the broader message is that forward-deployed capital and developer bandwidth are moving toward multifamily-integrated projects in growing Austin submarkets, not traditional office.

stories.title: Brixmor Picks Up Two Texas Shopping Centers
stories.excerpt: In this year’s second quarter, Brixmor Property Group Inc. acquired two Texas retail centers. The company paid $46.5 million for Jones Crossing in College Station, Texas, home to Texas A&M University. The center is anchored by an H-E-B market and has 15 acres of undeveloped land at its center. Stratus Properties sold the College Station property to Brixmor. The company also acquired Vintage Marketplace, an approximately 72,000-square-foot grocery-anchored neighborhood center in the northwest suburbs of Houston, Texas, for $32.7 million. Anchored by Whole Foods Market, the property complements 26 other Brixmor assets in the Houston market. The company cited near-term leasing of vacancies and below-market in-place rents as opportunities for value creation. In the first six months of the year, Brixmor reported the disposition of six shopping centers. The company generated gross proceeds of $123 million on the sales. Brixmor owns 344 open-air shopping centers totaling 62 million square feet nationwide. The post Brixmor Picks Up Two Texas Shopping Centers appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Brixmor acquired Jones Crossing (College Station, 15 acres, H-E-B anchor) for $46.5M from Stratus Properties
Brixmor also bought Vintage Marketplace (72K SF, Whole Foods anchor, NW Houston) for $32.7M in 2Q26
Portfolio now 344 open-air centers, 62M SF; Brixmor cited vacancy leasing and below-market rents as value drivers
stories.summary: Brixmor Property Group acquired two Texas retail centers in 2Q26: Jones Crossing in College Station (15 acres, H-E-B anchored, $46.5M from Stratus Properties) and Vintage Marketplace in NW Houston (72,000 SF, Whole Foods anchored, $32.7M). Both acquisitions target near-term leasing upside and below-market rent spreads. Brixmor's portfolio now includes 344 open-air centers totaling 62 million square feet.
stories.impact: Minimal relevance to multifamily development. REIT acquisition of grocery-anchored neighborhood retail reflects broader consumer spending resilience and retail recovery, but does not directly affect apartment demand, supply, or construction economics. College Station (Texas A&M) and Houston suburb assets may indirectly support populations feeding local student and suburban rental demand.

stories.title: Cavan Spins Off Phoenix BTR Community for $112.5M
stories.excerpt: Cavan Cos. has sold The Bungalows on Camelback, a 334-unit, build-to-rent asset in Phoenix, for $112.5 million. That comes out to about $337,000 a unit. A private individual acquired the property. This deal marked the biggest single-asset BTR sale in the metro’s history. Located on more than 29 acres at 4747 N. 99th Ave., The Bungalows on Camelback is less than 1 mile from Loop 101, which connects to Interstate 10 within roughly 3 miles. Completed in 2022, the property features one- to three-bedroom homes ranging between 674 and 1,355 square feet. Community amenities include a clubhouse, swimming pool, dog park and washing station, among others. Cavan Cos. debuted its BTR developments in 2016 through its Bungalow model. The company’s portfolio includes more than 100 projects and some 5,500 BTR units. Outside of Arizona, Cavan expanded to Omaha, Neb., in 2024 and Kansas City, Mo., this year. The company also looks to enter Santa Fe, N.M. The post Cavan Spins Off Phoenix BTR Community for $112.5M appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Cavan Cos. sold The Bungalows on Camelback (334-unit BTR, Phoenix) to private buyer for $112.5M ($337K/unit)
Largest single-asset BTR sale in Phoenix metro history; property on 29 acres near Loop 101, completed 2022
Cavan expanding BTR model beyond Arizona: Omaha (2024), Kansas City (2025), Santa Fe planned
stories.summary: Cavan Cos. sold The Bungalows on Camelback, a 334-unit build-to-rent community in Phoenix completed in 2022, for $112.5 million ($337,000 per unit) to a private buyer. The transaction marks the largest single-asset BTR sale in Phoenix metro history. Located on 29+ acres near Loop 101, the property features one- to three-bedroom homes with amenities including clubhouse, pool, and dog park. Cavan is expanding its BTR model to Omaha, Kansas City, and Santa Fe.
stories.impact: STRONG CAPITAL SIGNAL FOR BTR SECTOR AND PHOENIX MARKET. $337K/unit exit validates Cavan's BTR thesis and demonstrates investor appetite for single-family rental as liquid alternative to traditional multifamily. Phoenix market fundamentals strong enough to support trophy-asset single-owner takeout at premium valuation. Cavan's geographic expansion (Midwest, Southwest) signals confidence in secondary-market BTR economics and suggests BTR competition heating up outside California; developers should monitor cost and financing for BTR in tier-2 markets.

stories.title: REIT Values Are Hard to See Sometimes
stories.excerpt: Real estate investment trusts have earned a relatively clean bill of health in 2026, so far. After a prolonged period of underperformance, the investment vehicles for publicly traded real estate — widely known as REITs — are finally outperforming the S&P 500, with year-to-date total returns outpacing the index by more than 5 percentage points as of late June. But, despite the recent rebound, many REIT execs still don’t think the market has truly captured their value. Matt DiLiberto, chief financial officer at the New York City-based office titan SL Green, is among them. SL Green is having a banner year, benefiting from New York City’s record-high rents and limited vacancy among the company’s Class A office assets. Despite this, the company calculates that its stock price sits at a discount to the total value of its buildings — although SL Green’s calculations and those of analysts disagree on exactly how wide that gap stretches. “I really think it comes down to the inherent difference in how public investors trade — or invest in stocks, but I’ll call it trading because it’s not really investing — and how private market investors truly invest directly in real estate,” DiLiberto said. “It’s down to one thing: What is your investment horizon?” Those earnings-focused investors represent a growing share of REIT stock ownership, tilting the scales further toward near returns. Not to mention, the investment world got pretty distracted by the artificial intelligence boom in 2025. “If you’re trying to sell a 10- to 15-year investment vehicle to somebody who is trying to make a buck in three to six months, inherently there’s going to be a mismatch,” DiLiberto said. Whether the cause of the mismatch is interest rate fluctuations or property fundamentals, the industry and its investors agree on one thing: REIT values are an increasingly tough nut to crack. A REIT offers anyone the chance to be a passive investor in real estate via the stock market as opposed to investing di
bullets: REITs outperforming S&P 500 by 5+ percentage points YTD 2026; office REIT SL Green benefits from NYC record-high rents
SL Green calculates its stock trades at discount to net asset value (NAV); CFO DiLiberto cites public market trading vs. private capital divergence
SL Green Class A office portfolio tight on vacancy; public investors 'trading' vs. private market 'investing'
stories.summary: REITs have outperformed the S&P 500 by over 5 percentage points year-to-date in 2026. SL Green, a NYC office-focused REIT, is benefiting from record-high Manhattan rents and limited Class A vacancy. However, SL Green's CFO contends the stock trades at a discount to the underlying real estate's true value, attributing the gap to public market trading behavior versus private investor conviction. Public and private market valuations remain diverged.
stories.impact: MIXED SIGNAL FOR MULTIFAMILY DEVELOPMENT FINANCING AND EXIT STRATEGY. REIT outperformance suggests improving appetite for stabilized real estate equity and may unlock REIT-sponsored development financing pipelines. However, SL Green's NAV discount and public-private valuation gap caution that REIT equity multiples remain volatile and exit timing is uncertain. For developers seeking REIT partnerships or planning stabilized sales, public market sentiment is improving but not uniformly confident; private capital remains selective on pricing.

stories.title: South Florida Dirt: A timeline of the Vacchi vs. Stern legal battle
stories.excerpt: Developer Michael Stern teamed up with Italian investor and TikToker Gianluca Vacchi in 2024.  At the time, Stern said that Vacchi “understands new media and how to cut through the noise on social media in a way most real estate people don’t,” calling him an effective marketer and tastemaker. Online to his 22.1 million Instagram followers and 22.3 million TikTok followers, Vacchi has showcased his jetsetting lifestyle, workouts, cold plunges, and maybe most frequently, his dancing.  Two years ago, Stern and Vacchi said they were 50-50 partners on a $4 billion pipeline of JDS Development’s projects (Stern’s firm). That included […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Michael Stern (JDS Development) and Gianluca Vacchi ($4B pipeline) partnership imploded; Vacchi suing over Ponzi scheme allegations
Vacchi alleges Stern misrepresented projects (Mercedes-Benz Places, Dolce & Gabbana 888 Brickell, 1250 West Ave) and diverted funds
JDS exiting 1250 West Avenue; Mercedes-Benz and D&G projects stalled in litigation; JDS securing new debt and Jeff Soffer as partner
stories.summary: Developer Michael Stern's 50-50 partnership with Italian social-media investor Gianluca Vacchi on a $4 billion project pipeline has collapsed. Vacchi is suing Stern, alleging a Ponzi scheme and misrepresentation of project status, claiming promised investments were diverted to other developments. Projects including Mercedes-Benz Places Miami, Dolce & Gabbana-branded 888 Brickell, and 1250 West Avenue remain stalled. JDS is seeking new financing and has brought in developer Jeff Soffer as a partner.
stories.impact: CAUTIONARY SIGNAL FOR SOUTH FLORIDA CAPITAL AND EXECUTIVE DUE DILIGENCE. High-profile developer litigation over fund diversion and project misrepresentation raises governance and LP risk concerns for Miami trophy-asset development. Projects backed by social-media influencers as co-investors introduce reputational and capital stability risk; lenders and equity partners should stress-test sponsor track records and fund controls. South Florida condo/luxury market may face temporary headwinds from stalled projects and reputational spillover; watch for capital flight from JDS and similar structures.

stories.title: Mark Cuban takes Adelson to court on Mavs plans for new arena
stories.excerpt: Who could’ve guessed that selling a basketball team would change who’s in charge? Apparently not Mark Cuban.  After hinting at his regret over selling the Dallas Mavericks to Las Vegas Sands on a podcast in March, the billionaire businessman isn’t just going public with his concerns about the team’s new ownership–he’s taking them to court.  Cuban, who sold his majority stake in the team to Miriam Adelson’s casino company in 2023, claims in a new court filing that he was shut out of decisions about the team’s new arena, the Dallas Morning News reported. He’s asking the court to grant […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Mark Cuban sues Las Vegas Sands/Miriam Adelson over Dallas Mavericks arena development (Valley View Mall site)
Cuban claims verbal agreement to oversee operations while Sands pursued casino development, now shut out of decisions
Casino-arena contingent on Texas legalizing gambling; Dončić trade dispute cited as relationship nail-in-coffin
stories.summary: Mark Cuban, who sold his majority stake in the Dallas Mavericks to Las Vegas Sands (Miriam Adelson) in 2023, is suing to gain review rights over the team's planned arena development at the Valley View Mall site. Cuban alleges a verbal agreement to continue overseeing operations while Sands pursued casino development, but claims he was excluded from the Valley View acquisition decision. The casino-arena project remains contingent on Texas legalizing gambling.
stories.impact: MINIMAL DIRECT RELEVANCE TO MULTIFAMILY DEVELOPMENT. Sports franchise and casino-gaming land use are outside multifamily scope. However, the Valley View Mall site redevelopment—if stalled due to litigation—may delay mixed-use regeneration and indirectly affect Dallas' urban infill pipeline. Monitor for resolution; if Adelson/Sands move forward despite Cuban's challenge, the precedent could unlock additional major Dallas development sites tied up in corporate uncertainty.

stories.title: Nonprofit pitches housing conversion at Palo Alto site with SB 79 proposal
stories.excerpt: A small office building in Palo Alto could give way to a multifamily building not far from one of the city’s Caltrain stops.  San Mateo-based nonprofit Minority Television Project proposed the plan to erect a six-story building at 555 College Avenue with 70 apartments and roughly 8,000 square feet of ground-floor commercial space, Mercury News reported. The nonprofit purchased the property in 2023 for $7.1 million, according to documents filed with the Santa Clara County Recorder’s Office.  The building would consist of five stories of housing above a ground floor that could accommodate retail, dining or other commercial uses. The […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Minority Television Project proposes 70-unit, 6-story apartment building at 555 College Ave, Palo Alto via SB 79
Property near California Avenue Caltrain station; nonprofit purchased site in 2023 for $7.1M; also proposing 24-unit conversion at 127 Lytton
SB 79 allows taller transit-oriented housing near qualifying major transit hubs (effective start 2026)
stories.summary: San Mateo-based nonprofit Minority Television Project proposes a six-story, 70-unit apartment building at 555 College Avenue in Palo Alto, paired with 8,000 square feet of ground-floor commercial space. The project, filed under California Senate Bill 79, leverages the law's transit-oriented development provisions to exceed typical height limits near the California Avenue Caltrain station. The nonprofit purchased the property in 2023 for $7.1 million and is also pursuing a 24-unit office conversion at 127 Lytton Avenue.
stories.impact: MODERATE RELEVANCE TO BAY AREA MULTIFAMILY SUPPLY AND REGULATORY ENVIRONMENT. SB 79 is a supply-unlocking tool that will compress entitlement timelines and allow density near transit across Bay Area. Nonprofit development may crowd out for-profit sites, but SB 79's streamlined approval process could catalyze infill multifamily pipelines in Palo Alto and adjacent transit corridors. For-profit developers should monitor AI-driven labor demand in Silicon Valley and prioritize SB 79 sites to capture housing demand before scarcity inflates rents further; timing is critical for pre-emptive development.

stories.title: Steel IQ’s 3D-printed steel could slash builds from months to weeks
stories.excerpt: The new Lake Dallas startup Steel IQ believes that its 3D digital extrusion and printing is the sustainable future for affordable housing in America.  One of the major problems in the United States’ development pipeline is a lack of skilled construction workers. Steel IQ’s 3D printed steel frames may cost more to install, but require fewer skilled workers and cut down on framing timelines. The process uses a  digital model of a building, finds its dimensions with AI assistance and prints it in steel, down to the holes for the screws, according to the Dallas Morning News. Steel IQ’s founder […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Steel IQ (Lake Dallas startup) offers 3D-printed steel framing; reduces construction timeline from months to weeks, labor needs
System uses digital model + AI to print steel frames with screw holes; assembled on-site like Legos
Cost offset by waste reduction, shorter timelines, fewer skilled workers; resistant to adverse weather; zero-tree environmental benefit
stories.summary: Lake Dallas-based startup Steel IQ is developing a 3D digital extrusion and printing system for steel-framed construction. The process uses AI-assisted digital models to print customized steel frames with pre-drilled screw holes, reducing framing timelines from months to weeks and cutting skilled labor requirements. While steel costs more than wood, the efficiency gains (waste reduction, labor savings, timeline compression) offset the material premium. The framing is weather-resistant and environmentally advantageous (zero deforestation).
stories.impact: SIGNIFICANT FOR DEVELOPMENT FEASIBILITY AND COST IN LABOR-CONSTRAINED MARKETS. 3D-printed steel framing directly addresses multifamily construction bottleneck (skilled labor shortage) and could accelerate delivery timelines, particularly for mid-rise and garden-style products. For affordable-housing developers, labor-cost savings may unlock feasibility at lower rents; for luxury, faster stabilization improves ROI. Texas and Southwest markets facing labor constraints should pilot Steel IQ; if cost parity or premium is under 3–5%, adoption could unlock delayed pipelines and compress carry costs.

stories.title: There are no offices being built in Philadelphia
stories.excerpt: The drip of office developments springing up in Philadelphia has stilled, at least for now. Following the completion of a 438,000-square-foot building at 2000 Arch Street in the second quarter, there are no office projects in the city’s active pipeline, Bisnow reported. Chubb’s built-to-suit development was the only one still being developed, according to JLL. The lack of projects going up in the City of Brotherly Love stems in part from a dearth of tenants willing to anchor a space years before it’s actually available. “That’s been a really big hurdle when it comes to attracting out-of-market tenants,” JLL Philadelphia […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Philadelphia office pipeline zeroed out after 438K-SF Chubb built-to-suit delivery in 2Q26; no active projects remain
2Q26 vacancy dropped to 19.8% in CBD; absorption 492K SF mostly from Chubb; sublease inventory down 1M SF YoY
JLL cites lack of anchor tenants pre-leasing as major impediment; office inventory fell 2M+ SF YoY; few conversions announced
stories.summary: Following the completion of Chubb's 438,000-square-foot built-to-suit development at 2000 Arch Street in 2Q26, Philadelphia has zero office projects in its active development pipeline. The CBD vacancy rate fell to 19.8% in 2Q26, with 492,000 square feet of absorption—predominantly from Chubb. Declining inventory (down 2+ million square feet YoY) and reduced sublease space (down 1 million SF YoY) reflect market tightness, but the lack of pre-leased tenants is a major hurdle for new development. Suburban leasing slowed 43% YoY in 2Q26.
stories.impact: LIMITED RELEVANCE TO MULTIFAMILY, BUT SIGNALS BROADER MARKET BIFURCATION. Philadelphia's office collapse and zero-pipeline signal is evidence of structural office decline and tenant reluctance to commit to new space; this does NOT directly drive apartment demand. However, it underscores why multifamily developers should focus on transit-adjacent, mixed-use infill (office conversion, ground-floor retail) rather than office-dependent submarkets. If Philadelphia begins office-to-residential conversions as a desperate supply play, competition for conversion sites and financing may tighten; monitor for regulatory zoning/tax changes enabling conversions.
